sigma_fundamentals.title: Sigma Rule Fundamentals
sigma_fundamentals.lesson:
Sigma is an open, generic format for writing detection rules that describe how to find suspicious activity in log data. The same rule can, in principle, be translated into the query language of many different SIEM and log platforms — instead of writing the same detection logic over and over in each tool's own syntax. That's Sigma's core value: write once, deploy everywhere. This platform even includes a transpiler that converts your rule directly to Splunk SPL and Microsoft Sentinel KQL.

Every Sigma rule is written in YAML and has three parts that matter for this lab: a logsource (what kind of log data the rule applies to), a detection block (the actual matching logic), and a condition (how to combine multiple pieces of matching logic into one true/false decision).

Inside detection, you define one or more named selections — each selection is a set of field: value pairs that must ALL be true for that selection to match (this is an implicit AND). If you give a field a LIST of values instead of one, ANY of them matching is enough — for example, LogonType: [3, 9] matches if LogonType is 3 OR 9.

By default, matching a field's value requires an exact match. To match more flexibly, Sigma modifiers change how the comparison works. This lab supports: |contains (the field's value contains this substring anywhere), |endswith (the field's value ends with this string — useful for full file paths where you only care about the filename), |startswith, and |re (regular expression matching).

Finally, the condition field combines your named selections using and, or, not, and parentheses for grouping — for example, condition: selection and not filter means "match everything selection catches, except entries that also match filter." This is exactly how you exclude known-legitimate activity that would otherwise look identical to what you're hunting for — which is precisely what several of the challenges in this lab require you to do.

quiz.question: In a Sigma detection block, what does giving a field a list of values (e.g. LogonType: [3, 9]) mean?
options: The field must equal exactly the list itself
ANY of the listed values matching is enough (OR logic)
ALL of the listed values must match simultaneously
The field is ignored entirely
quiz.explanation: A list of values under a single field name is Sigma's shorthand for OR logic — the field matches if it equals any one of the listed values.

quiz.question: What does the |endswith modifier do?
options: Matches only if the field is completely empty
Matches if the field's value ends with the given string
Matches if the field's value starts with the given string
Reverses the field's value before matching
quiz.explanation: |endswith is especially useful for full file paths, where you only care about the filename at the end — for example, matching anything ending in \powershell.exe regardless of which folder it ran from.

quiz.question: Inside a single selection block, how are multiple field:value pairs combined?
options: OR — any one of them being true is enough
AND — all of them must be true
They're evaluated in random order
Only the first field listed is actually checked
quiz.explanation: A selection block is an implicit AND of everything inside it — every field:value pair listed must be true for that selection to match a given log entry.

quiz.question: What does condition: selection and not filter mean?
options: Match everything filter catches, except what selection also catches
Match everything selection catches, except entries that also match filter
Match only entries that match neither selection nor filter
This is invalid Sigma syntax
quiz.explanation: This is the standard pattern for excluding known-legitimate activity that would otherwise look identical to what you're hunting for — selection casts a wide net, filter carves out the false positives.

quiz.question: Why is Sigma often described as 'write once, deploy everywhere'?
options: Because Sigma rules automatically fix bugs in any SIEM
Because the same rule can be translated into different SIEM query languages instead of rewriting detection logic per platform
Because Sigma only works with Windows logs
Because Sigma rules never need to be updated once written
quiz.explanation: Rather than writing the same detection logic separately in Splunk SPL, Microsoft Sentinel KQL, and other platforms' native syntax, Sigma provides one common format that can be converted to many targets — this lab's own transpiler feature is a direct example of that.

challenges.title: Pass-the-Hash Detection
hints: Windows Event ID 4624 records every successful logon — the technique lives in the auth method and logon type, not the event ID alone.
Pass-the-Hash relies on NTLM instead of Kerberos, and typically arrives over the network rather than an interactive session.

Not every NTLM network logon in this dataset is an attack — there are at least two distinct legitimate reasons (e.g., legacy infrastructure and maintenance accounts). A rule that only checks the technique signature will over-match; you need a compound condition to carve out both known-legitimate cases.
challenges.lesson: Pass-the-Hash is a credential attack where an adversary uses a stolen NTLM password hash directly to authenticate — without ever knowing or cracking the actual plaintext password. In a healthy enterprise Windows domain, authentication should normally use Kerberos, not NTLM. When you see a network logon (LogonType 3) or NewCredentials logon (LogonType 9) authenticated via the legacy NTLM package instead of Kerberos, that's a signal worth investigating — it can indicate a hash-based attack, though it can also indicate legacy systems that haven't been migrated off NTLM. A good detection rule needs to account for both possibilities.

quiz.question: What makes Pass-the-Hash different from a typical password-based attack?
options: It requires physical access to the machine
It uses a stolen password hash directly, without ever knowing the plaintext password
It only works over HTTPS
It requires the victim to click a phishing link
quiz.explanation: Pass-the-Hash attacks work because NTLM authentication only requires the hash itself, not the original password — so an attacker who steals just the hash can authenticate without ever cracking or knowing the actual plaintext password.

quiz.question: In a modern Windows domain, which protocol should normally be used for logons instead of NTLM?
options: Kerberos
LDAP
RADIUS
SAML
quiz.explanation: Kerberos is the default, more secure authentication protocol in a modern Windows Active Directory domain. NTLM is a legacy protocol kept mainly for backward compatibility, and its continued use is often worth investigating.

quiz.question: Why is LogonType 3 combined with NTLM often worth investigating in an enterprise environment?
options: Network logons should never happen at all
Enterprise environments typically default to Kerberos, so an NTLM fallback can indicate an attack or an unmigrated legacy system
NTLM is always malicious with no exceptions
It indicates a printer configuration error
quiz.explanation: Well-configured enterprise domains prefer Kerberos, so seeing NTLM used for a network logon suggests either an attack (hash-based authentication) or a legacy/misconfigured system — both worth a SOC analyst's attention.

challenges.title: Scheduled Task Creation
hints: EventID 4698 fires on every scheduled task creation, legitimate or not — task creation itself is not the signal.
Attackers use scheduled tasks as a persistence mechanism, often to run something they would rather not type directly into a visible command line.
Look at what the task actually runs. Beware: there are multiple legitimate tasks that also use command-line encoding or LOLBins. You must filter out at least two independent legitimate tasks.
challenges.lesson: Windows Task Scheduler is a common persistence mechanism: an attacker who has gained initial access creates a scheduled task so their payload keeps running even after a reboot or logoff. To evade simple antivirus signatures, attackers often route the actual payload through 'living-off-the-land binaries' (LOLBins) — legitimate Windows executables like rundll32.exe or mshta.exe — or through base64-encoded PowerShell, so the malicious intent isn't obvious from a casual glance at the command line. Event ID 4698 logs every scheduled task creation, legitimate or not — task creation itself isn't the signal, what the task actually runs is.

quiz.question: Why do attackers commonly use Scheduled Tasks?
options: To slow down the target system
To establish persistence so their payload survives reboot or logoff
To bypass network firewalls specifically
To send phishing emails
quiz.explanation: Scheduled tasks let malicious code automatically re-run after a reboot, logoff, or at a set interval, without the attacker needing to maintain an active connection — that's exactly what 'persistence' means in an attack.

quiz.question: What is a 'living-off-the-land binary' (LOLBin)?
options: Custom malware written from scratch
A legitimate, pre-installed Windows executable that attackers repurpose to blend in
A type of firewall rule
An antivirus signature
quiz.explanation: rundll32.exe is a legitimate Windows system binary that attackers frequently abuse to execute malicious code indirectly, since its presence alone looks normal to defenders and simple signature-based tools.

quiz.question: Why can't a detection rule treat 'EventID 4698 exists' alone as malicious?
options: Because Windows doesn't actually log 4698 reliably
Because legitimate IT automation and software updaters also create scheduled tasks constantly
Because 4698 only applies to Linux systems
Because scheduled tasks are never logged
quiz.explanation: Windows itself, software updaters, and IT automation scripts all create scheduled tasks as part of completely normal operation — Event ID 4698 alone can't distinguish malicious activity from routine maintenance; the task's actual content is what matters.

challenges.title: LSASS Access (Credential Dumping)
hints: Credential dumping tools need to read protected memory from a specific, well-known Windows process.
Sysmon logs process-to-process memory access with a numeric access-rights code. The value used by classic dumping tools is well documented.
Some legitimate administrative and security tools also request that same access right. You will need to filter out multiple legitimate tools to avoid false positives.
challenges.lesson: LSASS (Local Security Authority Subsystem Service) holds credentials and password hashes in memory while a Windows system is running — which makes it a prime target for credential-dumping tools like Mimikatz. Sysmon Event ID 10 logs any time one process opens a handle to another process, including the specific access rights requested. Mimikatz and similar tools typically request a well-documented combination of access rights (0x1010) when reading LSASS memory. But legitimate security tools — antivirus, EDR agents, even Task Manager's 'Create dump file' feature — also access lsass.exe, usually with different access rights. A rule that only checks 'something touched lsass.exe' will be full of false positives.

quiz.question: What is stored in LSASS memory that makes it valuable to attackers?
options: Browser bookmarks
Cached credentials and password hashes
Installed application list
Network configuration only
quiz.explanation: LSASS manages authentication on a live Windows system and holds credential material, including hashes, in memory while sessions are active — exactly what makes it valuable to an attacker trying to harvest credentials.

quiz.question: What does Sysmon Event ID 10 record?
options: File creation events
One process opening a handle to access another process
Outbound network connections
Windows registry changes
quiz.explanation: Sysmon Event ID 10 specifically logs 'ProcessAccess' — one process requesting a handle to open and interact with another process's memory, the mechanism credential-dumping tools rely on.

quiz.question: Why can't a detection rule treat 'something accessed lsass.exe' alone as malicious?
options: lsass.exe is never legitimately accessed by anything
Legitimate security tools also access lsass.exe, typically with different access rights than credential-dumping tools
lsass.exe doesn't generate any logs
Sysmon doesn't monitor lsass.exe at all
quiz.explanation: Antivirus, EDR agents, and even built-in Windows tools legitimately open handles to lsass.exe. What distinguishes malicious access is typically the specific access rights requested (like the 0x1010 pattern associated with tools like Mimikatz), not just that access happened.

challenges.title: Encoded PowerShell
hints: Attackers obfuscate PowerShell payloads to avoid simple keyword-based detection and to bypass casual log review.
PowerShell itself has a native command-line switch for accepting a payload in this obfuscated form — that switch is the signal, not the presence of powershell.exe itself.
Both attackers and legitimate automation (like endpoint management or admin scripts) use encoded PowerShell. You'll need to explicitly filter out multiple legitimate sources.
challenges.lesson: Attackers frequently base64-encode PowerShell payloads (using the -enc or -EncodedCommand flag) specifically to obfuscate the malicious code from casual log review and from simple signature-based detection tools that look for readable keywords. PowerShell itself is used constantly for completely legitimate system administration, so its mere presence in logs isn't suspicious — the encoding and obfuscation is the actual signal an analyst should focus on, not the tool itself.

quiz.question: Why do attackers encode PowerShell commands instead of running them in plaintext?
options: Encoded commands execute faster
To obfuscate the payload from casual review and simple signature-based detection
PowerShell requires encoding to run any command at all
To reduce network bandwidth usage
quiz.explanation: Base64-encoding a PowerShell payload hides the actual command text from anyone glancing at logs or from simple keyword-matching security tools, without changing what the code does when executed.

quiz.question: Which flag is commonly associated with encoded PowerShell execution?
options: -Command
-EncodedCommand / -enc
-File
-Verbose
quiz.explanation: PowerShell's -EncodedCommand flag (often shortened to -enc) tells PowerShell to base64-decode and execute the given string as a command — this specific flag is a strong, well-known signature of obfuscated execution.

quiz.question: Is the mere presence of powershell.exe in logs inherently suspicious?
options: Yes, PowerShell should never run in a healthy environment
No — PowerShell is used constantly for legitimate administration; encoding/obfuscation is the real signal
Yes, but only outside business hours
No, PowerShell logs are never reliable enough to use
quiz.explanation: PowerShell is one of the most heavily used legitimate administration tools in any Windows environment — flagging every instance would produce overwhelming false positives. The encoding/obfuscation specifically is the meaningful signal.

challenges.title: SQL Injection
hints: Think about what a web request looks like when someone is trying to make the backend database do something it should not.
Classic SQL injection payloads combine several recognizable fragments — a boolean tautology, a UNION-based data exfiltration attempt, or a comment sequence used to truncate the rest of a query.
Vulnerability scanners and some internal tools also send similar patterns for testing or authorized use. Filter out both independent sources of benign noise.
challenges.lesson: SQL Injection happens when untrusted user input gets concatenated directly into a SQL query without proper handling, letting an attacker alter the query's logic. Common patterns include boolean-tautology payloads like ' OR '1'='1' (designed to make a WHERE clause always evaluate true, often to bypass login checks), UNION SELECT statements (used to append an attacker-controlled query onto the original one, exfiltrating data from other tables), and comment sequences like -- (used to truncate the rest of a legitimate query). Real attack traffic often overlaps with legitimate traffic in surprising ways, so a robust detection rule usually needs to combine multiple indicators rather than matching on a single generic pattern.

quiz.question: What is the core weakness that enables SQL Injection?
options: Weak user passwords
Untrusted input being concatenated directly into SQL queries without proper handling
Slow database server hardware
Missing HTTPS encryption
quiz.explanation: SQL Injection is fundamentally an input-validation failure: when user-supplied data is inserted directly into a SQL query string instead of being properly parameterized or sanitized, an attacker can inject their own SQL logic.

quiz.question: What does a payload like ' OR '1'='1' typically attempt to achieve?
options: Delete the entire database
Create a condition that's always true, often to bypass authentication logic
Encrypt the database connection
Speed up query execution
quiz.explanation: ' OR '1'='1' appended to a WHERE clause makes the overall condition always evaluate to true regardless of the real values — commonly used to bypass login checks that compare a submitted password against a stored one.

quiz.question: What is UNION SELECT commonly used for in a SQL injection attack?
options: Combining an attacker-controlled query with the original one to exfiltrate additional data
Deleting all rows in a table
Creating a brand new database
Restarting the SQL service
quiz.explanation: UNION SELECT lets an attacker append the results of their own arbitrary SELECT query onto the results of the original, legitimate query — a classic technique for pulling data out of tables the application was never meant to expose.

challenges.title: Malicious Service Persistence
hints: Windows Event ID 7045 logs every new service creation — but normal administrative software also installs services continuously.
Adversaries often place service binaries in user-writeable paths like Temp or AppData, or launch encoded PowerShell scripts directly.

Beware of legitimate enterprise automation: IT audit tools and corporate backup services may also run from temporary directories or script wrappers. Filter out both specific benign service names to avoid false positives.
challenges.lesson: Windows Event ID 7045 logs the installation of a new service on the system. Adversaries often create new services to maintain persistent access or execute payloads with SYSTEM privileges. Legitimate software installation and updates also register new services, typically installing binaries into C:\Windows\System32 or C:\Program Files. When a service binary resides in user-writeable directories like \Temp\ or \AppData\, or invokes encoded PowerShell, it strongly indicates malicious persistence.

quiz.question: What does Windows Event ID 7045 log?
options: A user logging off
A new Windows service being installed on the host
A network share being created
A firewall rule modification
quiz.explanation: Event ID 7045 specifically records when a new service is installed in the System log, including the ServiceName and ServiceFileName.

quiz.question: Why are service paths pointing to \Temp\ or \AppData\ suspicious?
options: Services are physically unable to execute from those folders
Legitimate system services usually install into C:\Windows\System32 or C:\Program Files; user-writeable folders like Temp/AppData are commonly abused by non-admin attackers
Temp directories are automatically deleted every 5 minutes
They indicate a corrupt Windows installation
quiz.explanation: User-writeable folders do not require administrative privileges to write files to, making them a common staging ground for adversaries establishing service persistence.

quiz.question: Why is a compound condition (with filters) required when searching for suspicious service paths?
options: Sigma rules do not support single selection blocks
Legitimate enterprise tools (like IT audit agents or backup scripts) may also run from temporary paths or script wrappers; filtering them avoids false positives
Event ID 7045 logs cannot be parsed without a filter
To speed up SIEM query execution
quiz.explanation: Corporate IT automation frequently uses temporary folders for staging installer agents or diagnostic scripts — without filter exclusions, a detection rule will generate high false-positive rates.

challenges.title: Suspicious Service-Account Interactive Logon
hints: Windows Event ID 4624 records all successful logons — the key signal for service account abuse lies in the LogonType.

Service accounts (conventionally named with prefixes like svc_) are designed for automated background processes (LogonType 5) or network logons (LogonType 3), never interactive keyboard/desktop logons (LogonType 2).

Not every interactive logon by a service-formatted account is malicious — emergency breakglass accounts and dedicated staging console logons may be authorized exceptions. Filter out both benign exceptions to achieve maximum precision.
challenges.lesson: Service accounts are non-human accounts designed to run background services, automated tasks, or database processes. In Windows security event logs (Event ID 4624), LogonType 2 signifies an interactive keyboard/desktop logon. Legitimate service accounts should only perform service logons (LogonType 5) or network logons (LogonType 3). When an account adhering to service naming conventions (e.g., prefix svc_) performs a LogonType 2 interactive logon, it indicates an adversary abusing service credentials to log in interactively.

quiz.question: What does LogonType 2 indicate in Windows Event ID 4624?
options: A service background logon
An interactive keyboard/desktop logon at the local console
A network drive mapping logon
An unlock workstation event
quiz.explanation: LogonType 2 is assigned when a user logs on interactively at the computer keyboard/terminal.

quiz.question: Why should service accounts (e.g., svc_sql) generally NOT perform LogonType 2 logons?
options: Service accounts are forbidden by Windows from logging on at all
Service accounts are intended for automated background services (LogonType 5) or network tasks (LogonType 3); interactive logons suggest human misuse or stolen credentials
LogonType 2 consumes double the RAM of LogonType 5
LogonType 2 only works for local Administrator accounts
quiz.explanation: Non-human service accounts have no legitimate need to initiate interactive desktop sessions under standard operation, so an interactive logon indicates credential theft or policy violation.

quiz.question: Why might a SOC analyst need filter exclusions when hunting for interactive service account logons?
options: To exclude authorized exceptions like emergency breakglass accounts (svc_breakglass) or dedicated staging consoles
Because Event ID 4624 is unreliable
Because NTLM authentication breaks LogonType 2 filtering
To prevent database locks in the SIEM
quiz.explanation: Certain breakglass procedures or staging environments legitimately allow interactive service account access; filtering known authorized exceptions prevents false positive alerts.
